text:
First Families of Toledo Polonia 
By Rosemary A. Chorzempa 
 
   Before the American Civil War, there were few Polish immigrants in northwest Ohio. In 1866, Poles 
from a small area of Poznania west of Znin began to arrive in Toledo, Ohio. Three hundred families have 
been documented as immigrants to the Toledo area as of 1877, and no doubt there are more that did not 
leave a paper trail. A database which will be available on the PGSA website includes some or all of the following 
information about each family unit:  names and aliases, parents’ names of adult immigrants, 
birth/baptismal, marriage, and death/burial information, origins in Poland (parish and/or village names), 
ships’ names and arrival dates, some information about children, residence (parish names, street names 
and neighborhoods in Toledo or other cities), occupations, known migrations from Toledo to other cities 
in the U.S., and known remarriages.

Three hundred families have 
been documented as immigrants to the Toledo area as of 1877, and no doubt there are more that did not 
leave a paper trail. A database which will be available on the PGSA website includes some or all of the following 
information about each family unit:  names and aliases, parents’ names of adult immigrants, 
birth/baptismal, marriage, and death/burial information, origins in Poland (parish and/or village names), 
ships’ names and arrival dates, some information about children, residence (parish names, street names 
and neighborhoods in Toledo or other cities), occupations, known migrations from Toledo to other cities 
in the U.S., and known remarriages. A Brief History of Poland for Genealogists 
 
   There were many reasons for Polish emigration. Studying Polish and European history and geography 
can shed a lot of light on why your ancestors settled where and when they did. By 1800, Poland no longer existed as a separate state, its vast area being divided up during three 
separate partitions by Russia, Prussia (Germany) and Austria.

Studying Polish and European history and geography 
can shed a lot of light on why your ancestors settled where and when they did. By 1800, Poland no longer existed as a separate state, its vast area being divided up during three 
separate partitions by Russia, Prussia (Germany) and Austria. Poland did not exist as a separate nation for 
over 120 years, until after World War I. While checking various documents, you may find your ancestors 
giving these three countries, or Russian-Poland, Prussian-Poland, German-Poland, Austrian-Poland or 
Galicia, as their origins. At times they may have also stated “Poland” as their birthplace.

While checking various documents, you may find your ancestors 
giving these three countries, or Russian-Poland, Prussian-Poland, German-Poland, Austrian-Poland or 
Galicia, as their origins. At times they may have also stated “Poland” as their birthplace. Poland was the breadbasket of Europe until about 1600 A.D. Polish wheat was of the highest quality in 
Europe. Poland exported many goods and crops to the rest of the continent.

Polish wheat was of the highest quality in 
Europe. Poland exported many goods and crops to the rest of the continent. The economy was very good, 
and even the peasant quality of life was better than it was in the next couple centuries. Then the climate 
cooled, called “the Little Ice Age” in Europe, lasting more than 200 years, until the mid-1800s. Droughts 
became more common.

Then the climate 
cooled, called “the Little Ice Age” in Europe, lasting more than 200 years, until the mid-1800s. Droughts 
became more common. Most people’s diets suffered from the wars and political and economic changes, 
even through the 1800s. By the 1850s, 80% of the population lived from agriculture. In the Prussian zone, 
where most of the earliest Polish immigrants came from, 55% of the residents relied on agriculture.

By the 1850s, 80% of the population lived from agriculture. In the Prussian zone, 
where most of the earliest Polish immigrants came from, 55% of the residents relied on agriculture. The 
population increased rapidly through the 1800s, even with mass emigration. The potato was introduced into the Polish diet in the early 1800s. It became the staple food of the 
growing number of town dwellers by the 1840s.

The potato was introduced into the Polish diet in the early 1800s. It became the staple food of the 
growing number of town dwellers by the 1840s. Poland and the rest of Europe was hit by the potato pest 
in the 1846-1855 time period, causing disease, starvation, an increase in the mortality rate and decrease in 
population. Asiatic cholera epidemics swept Poland and Europe in 1831, 1847-48, 1854, 1866, 1873, 1884 and 
1892. Uprisings and revolutions by the Poles against the occupying Prussians, Russians and Austrians 
occurred in 1830-31, 1844-48 and 1863.

Asiatic cholera epidemics swept Poland and Europe in 1831, 1847-48, 1854, 1866, 1873, 1884 and 
1892. Uprisings and revolutions by the Poles against the occupying Prussians, Russians and Austrians 
occurred in 1830-31, 1844-48 and 1863. Beginning in 1864, the Russification programs against the Poles intensified as a result of the January 
(1863) Uprising. Evidence of this can still be seen today when searching Catholic church records from the 
Russian-occupied areas. Instead of Latin, church records were required to be written in the Russian 
language.

Evidence of this can still be seen today when searching Catholic church records from the 
Russian-occupied areas. Instead of Latin, church records were required to be written in the Russian 
language. Beginning in 1867, Prime Minister Otto von Bismarck began his Kulturkampf. By 1871, his anti-
Catholic, anti-Polish plan was being implemented. More intense pressure was applied to Germanize the 
Poles.

By 1871, his anti-
Catholic, anti-Polish plan was being implemented. More intense pressure was applied to Germanize the 
Poles. Land was taken away from Polish farmers and sold to German immigrant farmers brought into the 
Polish lands by Bismarck. Many Catholic churches, monasteries and convents were closed, and some 
were converted to Evangelical (Lutheran) churches. Check LDS microfilms of records from your Polish 
parish for a parish history, or try online, as many parishes and towns in Poland now publish detailed 
online histories.

Many Catholic churches, monasteries and convents were closed, and some 
were converted to Evangelical (Lutheran) churches. Check LDS microfilms of records from your Polish 
parish for a parish history, or try online, as many parishes and towns in Poland now publish detailed 
online histories. The Slownik Geograficzny also contains parish histories in the town entries. The Austrian-occupied area, called Malopolska or Little Poland, was named Galicia by the Austrian 
government. (Wielkopolska, or Great Poland, was in the Prussian-occupied area.) Little Poland was the 
most underdeveloped area of the country with most of the population engaged in agriculture.

The Austrian-occupied area, called Malopolska or Little Poland, was named Galicia by the Austrian 
government. (Wielkopolska, or Great Poland, was in the Prussian-occupied area.) Little Poland was the 
most underdeveloped area of the country with most of the population engaged in agriculture. This area 
produced the greatest number of emigrants. Many Poles were conscripted into the occupying forces’ armies, and forced to fight in far-away wars. Conscription notations can be seen in many Catholic Church baptismal record books next to the male 
children’s birth records.

Many Poles were conscripted into the occupying forces’ armies, and forced to fight in far-away wars. Conscription notations can be seen in many Catholic Church baptismal record books next to the male 
children’s birth records. These notations were written in German script in the margins, probably by the 
draft board, and added many years after the births occurred when the young men were of draft age. A combination of all the above events caused the mass emigration of Poles. Polish emigrants not only 
came to the United States, but also to Canada, South America, France, England and Australia.

A combination of all the above events caused the mass emigration of Poles. Polish emigrants not only 
came to the United States, but also to Canada, South America, France, England and Australia. There were 
about 110,000 emigrants (total) who had left Poland by 1870. By 1914, there were about 4.2 million. Polish Immigration to Toledo 
 
   The earliest identified Polish immigrants to Toledo were Peter Szynka (aka Schinkey, Shinke) born 
about 1826 and his first wife Catherine Wisniewska b about 1830.

By 1914, there were about 4.2 million. Polish Immigration to Toledo 
 
   The earliest identified Polish immigrants to Toledo were Peter Szynka (aka Schinkey, Shinke) born 
about 1826 and his first wife Catherine Wisniewska b about 1830. Peter Schenk 28 a watchmaker and his 
wife Catherina 24 sailed on the SS Robert Peel from London arriving in New York on 22 November 
1854. Their only known child was Mary who was baptized in St. Joseph Church in Maumee, Ohio in 
November 1858; she married and had children.

Their only known child was Mary who was baptized in St. Joseph Church in Maumee, Ohio in 
November 1858; she married and had children. They were farmers and lived in Waynesfield Twp. in 
1870. By 1900, they lived in Maumee village on Sophia Street.

in 
1870. By 1900, they lived in Maumee village on Sophia Street. According to “Polacy w Toledo,” written in 1908 by Stefan Nesterowicz, Peter Szynka was from the 
village and parish of Tuchola near Naklo nad Notec (actually quite a few miles north of Naklo). Peter was 
the brother of Anna Szynka who married Jan Kortas. The Kortas family was also from Tuchola, and the 
families were related.

Peter was 
the brother of Anna Szynka who married Jan Kortas. The Kortas family was also from Tuchola, and the 
families were related. The Kalas family may also have been from Tuchola, and was also related to the 
Kortases. The Kortas and Kalas families immigrated in 1866 and 1868. Catherine Szynka/Shinkey died in 1894 and was buried in St.

The Kortas and Kalas families immigrated in 1866 and 1868. Catherine Szynka/Shinkey died in 1894 and was buried in St. Joseph Cemetery, Maumee. Peter married 
another Catherine later that same year at St. Hedwig Church.

Peter married 
another Catherine later that same year at St. Hedwig Church. His second wife was the widow of John 
Kledzik, her father was Maciej Chlopek. The second Mrs. Shinkey died in 1927, in Kuhschwantz.

The second Mrs. Shinkey died in 1927, in Kuhschwantz. Peter 
died 9 February 1918 in Monclova Twp, Ohio and is also buried in St. Joseph Cemetery. While there were a few individuals and their families who immigrated from Poland and lived in Toledo 
before 1870, they were mostly Jewish or German-Lutherans.

Joseph Cemetery. While there were a few individuals and their families who immigrated from Poland and lived in Toledo 
before 1870, they were mostly Jewish or German-Lutherans. A few Polish Catholics did arrive in Toledo 
in the late 1860s, but the mass immigration of Poles to Toledo finally commenced about the year 1870. According to author Stefan Nesterowicz, there were about 100 Polish families in Toledo in 1874. In 1880, 
there were 150 Polish families in the seventh ward (Lagrinka) and 80 families in the eighth ward 
(Kuhschwanz).

According to author Stefan Nesterowicz, there were about 100 Polish families in Toledo in 1874. In 1880, 
there were 150 Polish families in the seventh ward (Lagrinka) and 80 families in the eighth ward 
(Kuhschwanz). A small number of Polish immigrants served in Ohio regiments during the American Civil War. Some 
of these from northwest Ohio are noted in this First Families database. Some of these families who first settled in Toledo early on, moved to Manistee, Michigan and the 
Radom, Illinois area in the mid-1870s due to the poor economy in Toledo.

Some 
of these from northwest Ohio are noted in this First Families database. Some of these families who first settled in Toledo early on, moved to Manistee, Michigan and the 
Radom, Illinois area in the mid-1870s due to the poor economy in Toledo. A “clan” of related families 
originating in Lopienno parish, Poland moved to Radom (in the Belleville diocese of Illinois) and its 
surrounding farm communities. Those identified so far:  Anielak, Borowiak, Ciesliewicz, Czarnecki, 
Czerniejewski, Grzegorzewski, Kalas, Koczorowski, Kujawa, Lawecki, Martek, Musielak, Przybylski, 
Stoinski, Stelmaszewski, Szubarga (and Janowski), Wesolowski. Poles who lived in Toledo also had 
relatives in Fremont, Ohio (St.

Those identified so far:  Anielak, Borowiak, Ciesliewicz, Czarnecki, 
Czerniejewski, Grzegorzewski, Kalas, Koczorowski, Kujawa, Lawecki, Martek, Musielak, Przybylski, 
Stoinski, Stelmaszewski, Szubarga (and Janowski), Wesolowski. Poles who lived in Toledo also had 
relatives in Fremont, Ohio (St. Joseph), Marblehead, Ohio, and South Bend, Indiana. There were small 
enclaves of Poles in St. Boniface parish in Oak Harbor, Ohio (Ottawa County), Immaculate Conception 
parish in Port Clinton, Ohio (Ottawa County) and St.

There were small 
enclaves of Poles in St. Boniface parish in Oak Harbor, Ohio (Ottawa County), Immaculate Conception 
parish in Port Clinton, Ohio (Ottawa County) and St. Mary parish in Sandusky, Ohio (Erie County). As more and more Poles arrived in America, they wrote home to their families and friends back in the 
Old Country. Many adult siblings, and even families of three generations at a time, came to Toledo in the 
1870s and 1880s.

As more and more Poles arrived in America, they wrote home to their families and friends back in the 
Old Country. Many adult siblings, and even families of three generations at a time, came to Toledo in the 
1870s and 1880s. Some entries in the Slownik Geograficzny mention that the population of the entire village left for 
America. (Slownik Geograficzny = Geographical Dictionary of  Poland, a very useful reference available 
on a CD-rom from the PGSA, describing every village in the Polish territories, written in 1880-1900, the 
time of our ancestors’ emigration). Folwark Ameryka was the name of a large manor and farm near 
Szubin, an area from which many of the first Toledo Polonia came.

(Slownik Geograficzny = Geographical Dictionary of  Poland, a very useful reference available 
on a CD-rom from the PGSA, describing every village in the Polish territories, written in 1880-1900, the 
time of our ancestors’ emigration). Folwark Ameryka was the name of a large manor and farm near 
Szubin, an area from which many of the first Toledo Polonia came. They came from Poznania (Posen in German), specifically from the area bounded by Kamien Krajenski 
(just north of Bydgoszcz) on the north to Poznan on the south, and on the west Rosko (northwest of 
Poznan) to Inowroclaw on the east. Most of these pioneers came from the Szubin-Znin-Lopienno-Kcynia 
region, an area roughly 15 miles by 18 miles. The majority of them came from the parish towns of Znin, Szubin, Kcynia, Lopienno, Cerekwica, 
Dziewierzewo, Golancz, Czeszewo Wagrowiec, Sadki, Smogulec, Gorzyce, Labiszyn, Rogozno, 
Brzyskorzystew, Juncewo, Swiatkowo, Czewujewo, Rogowo, Trzemeszno, Poznan, Miescisko, Lekno, 
Rosko, Srebnagora, Slupy, Ryszewko, Lechlin, Ostrowite Prymasowskie, Popowo Koscielne, Podlesie 
Koscielne, Szaradowo, Witkowo, Czerniejewo, Kostrzyn, Gluchowo, Kwieciszewo, Inowroclaw, Bysla, 
Makowarsko, Blugowo, Glesno, and Gniezno.

Most of these pioneers came from the Szubin-Znin-Lopienno-Kcynia 
region, an area roughly 15 miles by 18 miles. The majority of them came from the parish towns of Znin, Szubin, Kcynia, Lopienno, Cerekwica, 
Dziewierzewo, Golancz, Czeszewo Wagrowiec, Sadki, Smogulec, Gorzyce, Labiszyn, Rogozno, 
Brzyskorzystew, Juncewo, Swiatkowo, Czewujewo, Rogowo, Trzemeszno, Poznan, Miescisko, Lekno, 
Rosko, Srebnagora, Slupy, Ryszewko, Lechlin, Ostrowite Prymasowskie, Popowo Koscielne, Podlesie 
Koscielne, Szaradowo, Witkowo, Czerniejewo, Kostrzyn, Gluchowo, Kwieciszewo, Inowroclaw, Bysla, 
Makowarsko, Blugowo, Glesno, and Gniezno. (See map...Ancestral towns & villages marked in red.) 
 
 
 
   Many villages in Poland were inhabited by as few as a dozen people to a hundred or two, and were too 
small to support their own parish and church. Churches were built in larger villages and in towns, and one 
parish may have served a dozen small villages surrounding it. Thus, the names of the villages and towns 
above are the location of the parish churches, and not necessarily the name of the immigrant’s village.

Churches were built in larger villages and in towns, and one 
parish may have served a dozen small villages surrounding it. Thus, the names of the villages and towns 
above are the location of the parish churches, and not necessarily the name of the immigrant’s village. When the immigrant was giving the pastor in America his information for his marriage record, for 
example, he was asked where he was baptized. These parish towns’ names were taken from our local 
Catholic church records and parish records from Poland. The oldest Polish settlement in America at Panna Maria, Texas was begun in 1854.

These parish towns’ names were taken from our local 
Catholic church records and parish records from Poland. The oldest Polish settlement in America at Panna Maria, Texas was begun in 1854. The Polish origins 
of these Texas immigrants is nearly identical to that of Toledo. Texas Poles came from the parishes of 
Margonin, Jaktorowo, Smogulec, Kcynia, Panigrodz, Czeszewo, Srebnagora, Dziewierzewo, Slupy, 
Szaradowo, Szubin, Skoki and Lechlin. The Early Polish Settlements in Toledo 
 
   Before the 1875 founding of St.

Texas Poles came from the parishes of 
Margonin, Jaktorowo, Smogulec, Kcynia, Panigrodz, Czeszewo, Srebnagora, Dziewierzewo, Slupy, 
Szaradowo, Szubin, Skoki and Lechlin. The Early Polish Settlements in Toledo 
 
   Before the 1875 founding of St. Hedwig, the first Polish Roman Catholic Church in Toledo, the Polish 
Catholics were served by the two German Catholic Churches in Toledo, St. Mary and Saints Peter and 
Paul. These Poles were familiar with the German language, as that was the official language of their 
homeland.

Mary and Saints Peter and 
Paul. These Poles were familiar with the German language, as that was the official language of their 
homeland. These two German churches also happened to be near the areas of the Polish settlements in 
Toledo, one to the north of downtown (Lagrinka) and one to the west of downtown (Kuhschwanz). Vistula (the Latin word for the Polish river Wisla) was a town platted out in 1833 and located in the Old 
North End, which merged with the rival town of Port Lawrence (immediately to the west of Vistula, the 
site of the current downtown Toledo) founded in 1817. Toledo arose from this merger in 1837.

Vistula (the Latin word for the Polish river Wisla) was a town platted out in 1833 and located in the Old 
North End, which merged with the rival town of Port Lawrence (immediately to the west of Vistula, the 
site of the current downtown Toledo) founded in 1817. Toledo arose from this merger in 1837. The first Poles in Toledo settled in the older southern portion of what is now known as “Lagrinka,” the 
Polish settlement in north Toledo, beginning in 1867. This area was first known as “the Polish 
Settlement” in the 1870s and 1880s. These first homes were located well to the south of the site of the 
first (and current) St.

This area was first known as “the Polish 
Settlement” in the 1870s and 1880s. These first homes were located well to the south of the site of the 
first (and current) St. Hedwig Church, in the area of Lagrange to Cherry streets, and Bancroft to Frederick 
streets. The largest cluster of Poles lived in and around the 900 block of Frederick Street. After St.

The largest cluster of Poles lived in and around the 900 block of Frederick Street. After St. Hedwig Church was built in 1875, the Poles settled in the area surrounding the first church, which was 
built on the north side of the 100 block of Dexter Street, near Warsaw Street (then called Locust Street). After the original church building was torn down, the property became Dexter Park, a city park, and its 
location is marked with a historical marker. By 1880, there were two distinct “Lagrinkas.” One, to the south of Frederick Street, and the newer one 
to the north of Hausman Street.

After the original church building was torn down, the property became Dexter Park, a city park, and its 
location is marked with a historical marker. By 1880, there were two distinct “Lagrinkas.” One, to the south of Frederick Street, and the newer one 
to the north of Hausman Street. The newer Polish Settlement surrounded St. Hedwig Church; most Poles 
lived in the 100 blocks of Mettler Street (then called Franklin), Everett and Dexter Streets, and Bronson 
Avenue. Two large tracts of land, privately owned, divided the two Lagrinkas, but were soon sold and 
eventually Poles and other residents moved into that area.

Hedwig Church; most Poles 
lived in the 100 blocks of Mettler Street (then called Franklin), Everett and Dexter Streets, and Bronson 
Avenue. Two large tracts of land, privately owned, divided the two Lagrinkas, but were soon sold and 
eventually Poles and other residents moved into that area. The Poles in north Toledo walked over a mile south down Cherry Street to St. Mary Church, which at 
that time was located on the west side of Cherry Street at Michigan, on the southern edge of the Wabash 
& Erie Canal bed (aka “the big ditch.” In Lucas Co. records, many people resided and/or died at the big 
ditch.) St.

Mary Church, which at 
that time was located on the west side of Cherry Street at Michigan, on the southern edge of the Wabash 
& Erie Canal bed (aka “the big ditch.” In Lucas Co. records, many people resided and/or died at the big 
ditch.) St. Mary was founded as a German Catholic Church in 1854. St. Mary Cemetery was located at the 
northwest corner of Lagrange Street and Manhattan Blvd.

St. Mary Cemetery was located at the 
northwest corner of Lagrange Street and Manhattan Blvd. As was the custom and the law, the cemetery 
was outside the city limits (until 1868 when the city annexed the area). St. Francis de Sales Church, 
organized in 1841 and located just three blocks to the south of St.

St. Francis de Sales Church, 
organized in 1841 and located just three blocks to the south of St. Mary Church, also had its cemetery in 
the same location. Eventually the two side-by-side cemeteries merged into Mount Carmel Cemetery. One 
can still find gravestones that date from the 1840s.

Eventually the two side-by-side cemeteries merged into Mount Carmel Cemetery. One 
can still find gravestones that date from the 1840s. No evidence of Polish baptisms, marriages or funerals are found in the early St. Francis de Sales records 
(at that time an Irish parish), nor those of St. Joseph Parish (then a French parish, organized in 1854), 
located about three blocks to the east of St.

Francis de Sales records 
(at that time an Irish parish), nor those of St. Joseph Parish (then a French parish, organized in 1854), 
located about three blocks to the east of St. Francis and St. Mary parishes, at Locust and Erie Streets. Places of employment for the earliest Polish settlers included the Milburn Wagon Works (Monroe & 
Detroit), Osborn, Chase and Swayne wood moulding company (Chestnut & Champlain), B.

Mary parishes, at Locust and Erie Streets. Places of employment for the earliest Polish settlers included the Milburn Wagon Works (Monroe & 
Detroit), Osborn, Chase and Swayne wood moulding company (Chestnut & Champlain), B. Meilink & 
Co. furniture (Summit & Cherry), Hubbard, Newhouse & Cable flooring (Lagrange & Water), Christian 
Guenther dyer and scourer (47 Adams Street), J. Brereton carpentry, William Peter carpentry, Finlay & 
Zahn, Buckeye Brewery (Bush & Champlain), Toledo Gas Light and Coke Co.

furniture (Summit & Cherry), Hubbard, Newhouse & Cable flooring (Lagrange & Water), Christian 
Guenther dyer and scourer (47 Adams Street), J. Brereton carpentry, William Peter carpentry, Finlay & 
Zahn, Buckeye Brewery (Bush & Champlain), Toledo Gas Light and Coke Co. (69 Madison), Lake Shore 
and Michigan Southern Railroad. By 1923, Poles were the largest ethnic group in the city of Toledo, generating the need for more parishes 
to serve them. Currently, there are about 60,000 residents of the Greater Toledo area who claim Polish 
ancestry and heritage.

By 1923, Poles were the largest ethnic group in the city of Toledo, generating the need for more parishes 
to serve them. Currently, there are about 60,000 residents of the Greater Toledo area who claim Polish 
ancestry and heritage. Kuhschwanz Settlement 
 
   In 1871, Poles began to settle in the area now known as the Nebraska Avenue area or “Kuhschwanz,” 
“Cow’s Tail” in German. It probably was named by the German immigrants living in this area of town 
known as Lenk’s Hill, a German settlement. It really was the “Cow’s Tail,” or as some might say “out in 
the sticks,” as this Polish settlement was literally on the edge of the Toledo city limits.

It probably was named by the German immigrants living in this area of town 
known as Lenk’s Hill, a German settlement. It really was the “Cow’s Tail,” or as some might say “out in 
the sticks,” as this Polish settlement was literally on the edge of the Toledo city limits. At that time, the 
spot where St. Anthony Church stands now was outside the Toledo city limits, in Adams Township. Some 
of these people farmed in the area.

Anthony Church stands now was outside the Toledo city limits, in Adams Township. Some 
of these people farmed in the area. The earliest Polish residents of Kuhschwanz lived in the area roughly bounded by Belmont, Detroit, 
Campbell and Hawley. This area was just to the west of the German Lenk’s Hill settlement, on the 
western edge of the city of Toledo and beyond. The Kuhschwanz Poles had to walk about a mile to the 
east, crossing Swan Creek and the Canal bed, to attend church services at SS.

This area was just to the west of the German Lenk’s Hill settlement, on the 
western edge of the city of Toledo and beyond. The Kuhschwanz Poles had to walk about a mile to the 
east, crossing Swan Creek and the Canal bed, to attend church services at SS. Peter & Paul Church. Many 
of the residents of this area were buried in St. Patrick’s Cemetery, according to Lucas County death 
records, though there is no record of such a cemetery ever being in existence, according to the Toledo 
Diocese.

Many 
of the residents of this area were buried in St. Patrick’s Cemetery, according to Lucas County death 
records, though there is no record of such a cemetery ever being in existence, according to the Toledo 
Diocese. St. Patrick Church was (and still is in the same location) in Lenk’s Hill. It was founded in 1862 
as an Irish parish.

Patrick Church was (and still is in the same location) in Lenk’s Hill. It was founded in 1862 
as an Irish parish. SS. Peter & Paul Church was founded in 1866 as a German parish and located on St. Clair Street near 
Harrison, near the canal locks and south of Swan Creek.

Peter & Paul Church was founded in 1866 as a German parish and located on St. Clair Street near 
Harrison, near the canal locks and south of Swan Creek. There are not many records of Poles in this 
parish until early 1873, when there were 15 baptisms of Polish infants that year. The Saints Peter & Paul 
Cemetery was at the intersection of Western and Wayne Streets, and served from 1867 to 1923. In the 
1920s the bodies were reinterred at Calvary Cemetery.

The Saints Peter & Paul 
Cemetery was at the intersection of Western and Wayne Streets, and served from 1867 to 1923. In the 
1920s the bodies were reinterred at Calvary Cemetery. The old Foodtown store at Western and Airport 
sits on this former cemetery property. The First Polish Roman Catholic Church in Toledo – St. Hedwig (October 16, 1875) 
 
   After a sufficient number of Polish families immigrated to Toledo, they sought to form a Polish Roman 
Catholic parish.

The First Polish Roman Catholic Church in Toledo – St. Hedwig (October 16, 1875) 
 
   After a sufficient number of Polish families immigrated to Toledo, they sought to form a Polish Roman 
Catholic parish. The residents of both Polish settlements received permission from the Bishop of the 
Cleveland Diocese. (The diocese of Toledo was not formed until 1910.) 
   October 16, 1875 marked the official beginning of St. Hedwig Parish with the celebration of the first 
Mass in the basement of St.

(The diocese of Toledo was not formed until 1910.) 
   October 16, 1875 marked the official beginning of St. Hedwig Parish with the celebration of the first 
Mass in the basement of St. Mary Church. Construction was begun shortly after, and the red brick church 
was built at 100 S. Dexter Street, between Locust (now Warsaw) and Lagrange Streets.

Construction was begun shortly after, and the red brick church 
was built at 100 S. Dexter Street, between Locust (now Warsaw) and Lagrange Streets. The church was 
blessed on May 7, 1876. Father Vincent Lewandowski was the first pastor 
Once St. Hedwig was established, almost all of the Polish families went to St.

Father Vincent Lewandowski was the first pastor 
Once St. Hedwig was established, almost all of the Polish families went to St. Hedwig for the sacraments. Records of Polish sacraments at St. Mary and SS.

Records of Polish sacraments at St. Mary and SS. Peter & Paul parishes became almost non-existent after 
1875. There was a fire in St. Hedwig Church in 1886, during which time the first book of death records 
disappeared.

There was a fire in St. Hedwig Church in 1886, during which time the first book of death records 
disappeared. That is part of the reason for including Lucas County death records in this project, as well as 
to include Poles who lived in Kuhschwanz or other parts of the city. After the fire, the second and final 
building was built in 1891, located at 1916 Lagrange Street, only one block away from the original St. Hedwig Church.

After the fire, the second and final 
building was built in 1891, located at 1916 Lagrange Street, only one block away from the original St. Hedwig Church. In 2005, St. Adalbert was twinned with St. Hedwig and shared a pastor.

Adalbert was twinned with St. Hedwig and shared a pastor. On 1 July 2010, St. Adalbert and St. Hedwig parishes were “suppressed,” a diocesan term that means 
each parish was terminated with all metrical records from both parishes sent to the diocese.

The Cedar Harbor Pilot is a small internal evaluation project created to test whether a local retrieval-augmented assistant can run reliably on older mobile hardware without using any cloud APIs. The project is managed by Neel Varma under the internal identifier CHP-3182 and officially began on 2025-10-14 at the West Annex Lab, Room C7. Its main goal is to compare lightweight local models for document search and grounded question answering using a limited offline document set. At the moment, the test collection contains 11 notes, 4 short reference guides, and 2 archived reports. The preferred answer model for the current phase is qwen3:0.6b, while the embedding model used for retrieval is embeddinggemma.

At the moment, the test collection contains 11 notes, 4 short reference guides, and 2 archived reports. The preferred answer model for the current phase is qwen3:0.6b, while the embedding model used for retrieval is embeddinggemma. The internal codename for the project is Silent Orbit, the budget ceiling is 2400 USD, and the scheduled review date is 2025-12-05. All indexed files are stored under /data/projects/cedar_harbor/, and the verification phrase recorded for internal testing is "amber circuit". The project is strictly experimental, not cleared for production deployment, and no external API calls are permitted.

All indexed files are stored under /data/projects/cedar_harbor/, and the verification phrase recorded for internal testing is "amber circuit". The project is strictly experimental, not cleared for production deployment, and no external API calls are permitted. The final summary document is supposed to be delivered to Leena Das after the evaluation is complete. No device serial numbers, email addresses, Wi-Fi credentials, or hardware chip details are included in this note.

Northbridge Biologics
Internal Operations Memorandum

Document ID: NBB-OPS-INT-042
Classification: Internal Use Only
Prepared by: Strategic Operations Office
Last Updated: February 12, 2026
Distribution: Operations, Product, Regulatory, IT Support, Customer Success

Subject: Q2 Readiness Plan for Project Harbor and Associated Workflow Changes

Overview

This memo provides a consolidated update on Project Harbor, the internal codename for Northbridge Biologics’ next-phase customer delivery framework. The purpose of Project Harbor is to reduce turnaround time for custom assay onboarding, improve internal visibility across fulfillment stages, and create a more consistent support experience for enterprise customers. This document summarizes the current operating assumptions, implementation milestones, cross-functional responsibilities, known risks, and communication expectations for the Q2 transition period. Project Harbor was initiated in response to three recurring operational issues identified during the Q4 and Q1 reviews. First, onboarding requests were being routed through multiple teams without a single ownership model, which caused delays and duplicate work.

Project Harbor was initiated in response to three recurring operational issues identified during the Q4 and Q1 reviews. First, onboarding requests were being routed through multiple teams without a single ownership model, which caused delays and duplicate work. Second, customer-facing timelines were often based on informal estimates rather than standardized capacity rules. Third, issue escalation from Customer Success to Product and Regulatory frequently lacked the minimum context required for rapid review. The Harbor initiative is intended to address these gaps through process simplification, structured handoff templates, and limited automation in internal ticketing.

Third, issue escalation from Customer Success to Product and Regulatory frequently lacked the minimum context required for rapid review. The Harbor initiative is intended to address these gaps through process simplification, structured handoff templates, and limited automation in internal ticketing. Scope

Project Harbor applies to all new enterprise assay onboarding requests submitted on or after April 1, 2026. Existing customer workflows will continue under the current model unless specifically migrated by Operations leadership. The new framework applies to the following departments: Commercial Intake, Product Operations, Regulatory Documentation, Laboratory Coordination, Data Systems, and Customer Success.

Existing customer workflows will continue under the current model unless specifically migrated by Operations leadership. The new framework applies to the following departments: Commercial Intake, Product Operations, Regulatory Documentation, Laboratory Coordination, Data Systems, and Customer Success. Finance and Legal are not direct owners of the Harbor workflow, but may be consulted for contract-dependent exceptions. The Harbor framework does not replace quality management procedures, change control requirements, or customer contract review. It is an operating layer intended to improve coordination before a project enters formal validation or regulated execution.

The Harbor framework does not replace quality management procedures, change control requirements, or customer contract review. It is an operating layer intended to improve coordination before a project enters formal validation or regulated execution. Teams should not interpret Harbor status updates as approvals for release, compliance signoff, or production readiness. Core Workflow Changes

Beginning in Q2, all onboarding requests must enter through the Intake Gateway form in ServiceHub rather than through email, chat, or spreadsheet tracking. Requests submitted outside ServiceHub should be redirected rather than manually processed.

Core Workflow Changes

Beginning in Q2, all onboarding requests must enter through the Intake Gateway form in ServiceHub rather than through email, chat, or spreadsheet tracking. Requests submitted outside ServiceHub should be redirected rather than manually processed. The Intake Gateway form requires five mandatory fields: customer name, request category, target launch date, region, and requested assay panel. Optional fields may be completed later, but the mandatory fields must be present for a submission to receive a Harbor tracking ID. Once submitted, each request will be automatically tagged using a two-level priority system.

Optional fields may be completed later, but the mandatory fields must be present for a submission to receive a Harbor tracking ID. Once submitted, each request will be automatically tagged using a two-level priority system. Priority level is determined by commercial commitment and implementation urgency. Tier 1 requests include signed enterprise expansions, regulated customer migrations, and executive-approved pilots. Tier 2 requests include exploratory opportunities, unsupported configuration requests, and general capability inquiries.

Tier 1 requests include signed enterprise expansions, regulated customer migrations, and executive-approved pilots. Tier 2 requests include exploratory opportunities, unsupported configuration requests, and general capability inquiries. Teams should note that Tier 1 designation accelerates review order but does not waive documentation requirements. After intake, the request will move through four standard checkpoints: triage, feasibility review, documentation review, and launch preparation. Each checkpoint has a designated owner.

After intake, the request will move through four standard checkpoints: triage, feasibility review, documentation review, and launch preparation. Each checkpoint has a designated owner. Triage is owned by Commercial Intake, feasibility review by Product Operations, documentation review by Regulatory Documentation, and launch preparation by Laboratory Coordination with support from Customer Success. A checkpoint may not be marked complete unless the required notes are entered in the Harbor activity log. System and Data Notes

The Harbor dashboard will be available through the internal Portal beginning March 25, 2026.

A checkpoint may not be marked complete unless the required notes are entered in the Harbor activity log. System and Data Notes

The Harbor dashboard will be available through the internal Portal beginning March 25, 2026. Access will initially be limited to managers, workflow owners, and assigned coordinators. General employee access will be expanded in a later phase after role-based filtering is validated. The dashboard is intended as the source of truth for request status, owner assignment, and expected completion dates.

General employee access will be expanded in a later phase after role-based filtering is validated. The dashboard is intended as the source of truth for request status, owner assignment, and expected completion dates. Teams should discontinue parallel status tracking in personal spreadsheets once Harbor is live. Data entered into Harbor should not include protected health information, raw customer datasets, or attachment files containing unredacted identifiers. Harbor is not a document repository for regulated source records.

Data entered into Harbor should not include protected health information, raw customer datasets, or attachment files containing unredacted identifiers. Harbor is not a document repository for regulated source records. Supporting files should continue to be stored in the approved ShareVault structure with the Harbor tracking ID referenced in the filename. IT Support will audit storage practices during the first six weeks of rollout to identify noncompliant usage. The current integration between Harbor and ServiceHub is one-way only.

IT Support will audit storage practices during the first six weeks of rollout to identify noncompliant usage. The current integration between Harbor and ServiceHub is one-way only. Updates entered in Harbor will not write back to historical tickets in ServiceHub. For this reason, coordinators must verify that customer-facing updates in CRM match the latest Harbor status before sending timeline commitments. A bidirectional sync is planned for a later release but has not yet passed internal validation.

For this reason, coordinators must verify that customer-facing updates in CRM match the latest Harbor status before sending timeline commitments. A bidirectional sync is planned for a later release but has not yet passed internal validation. Roles and Responsibilities

Department managers are responsible for ensuring that at least one primary and one backup owner are assigned for each Harbor checkpoint. Backup owners must be trained before March 28. Individual contributors are expected to update notes within one business day of completing a review step.

Backup owners must be trained before March 28. Individual contributors are expected to update notes within one business day of completing a review step. Delayed note entry creates downstream reporting errors and reduces forecast accuracy, which was a major issue in the previous operating model. Customer Success should not promise a launch date based only on verbal confirmation from a single department. A launch estimate may only be communicated after the request reaches documented feasibility review and receives a preliminary timing band in Harbor.

Customer Success should not promise a launch date based only on verbal confirmation from a single department. A launch estimate may only be communicated after the request reaches documented feasibility review and receives a preliminary timing band in Harbor. Timing bands are defined as Standard, Accelerated, or Complex. These are internal planning indicators and should not be copied verbatim into customer messaging without approved wording. Product Operations will maintain the list of supported assay configurations.

These are internal planning indicators and should not be copied verbatim into customer messaging without approved wording. Product Operations will maintain the list of supported assay configurations. Requests outside the supported matrix should be labeled Exception Review Required. Exception reviews are discussed during the Tuesday cross-functional operations meeting and require representation from Product, Regulatory, and Laboratory Coordination. If a request is not suitable for exception review, the decision and rationale must still be recorded in Harbor for reporting and future analysis.

Exception reviews are discussed during the Tuesday cross-functional operations meeting and require representation from Product, Regulatory, and Laboratory Coordination. If a request is not suitable for exception review, the decision and rationale must still be recorded in Harbor for reporting and future analysis. Known Risks

The largest near-term risk is partial adoption. If teams continue to manage requests through side channels, the dashboard will become incomplete and leadership reporting will be unreliable. The second major risk is overuse of Tier 1 classification.

If teams continue to manage requests through side channels, the dashboard will become incomplete and leadership reporting will be unreliable. The second major risk is overuse of Tier 1 classification. Managers should avoid inflating priority labels to bypass queue discipline, as this will reduce the credibility of the system and create conflict between departments. The third risk is inconsistent documentation quality, especially in feasibility review notes. Another operational concern is training variance across regions.

The third risk is inconsistent documentation quality, especially in feasibility review notes. Another operational concern is training variance across regions. North America and EU teams have already completed the pilot workflow walkthrough, but APAC onboarding leads will not complete training until late March. During the first two weeks of April, APAC requests may experience longer triage times while support coverage stabilizes. This should be reflected in internal planning but not framed as a system failure.

During the first two weeks of April, APAC requests may experience longer triage times while support coverage stabilizes. This should be reflected in internal planning but not framed as a system failure. Communication Guidance

Questions about process ownership should be directed to the Strategic Operations Office, while technical access issues should be routed to IT Support under the “Harbor Access” category. Employees should avoid creating informal FAQs in chat channels, since outdated answers tend to persist and create confusion. The official reference materials will be maintained in the Portal knowledge page under Operations > Workflow Standards > Project Harbor.

Employees should avoid creating informal FAQs in chat channels, since outdated answers tend to persist and create confusion. The official reference materials will be maintained in the Portal knowledge page under Operations > Workflow Standards > Project Harbor. Managers are encouraged to collect examples of ambiguous cases during the first month of rollout. These examples will be used to refine the training guide and improve decision rules for future automation. Feedback should be specific, documented, and tied to a Harbor tracking ID whenever possible.

These examples will be used to refine the training guide and improve decision rules for future automation. Feedback should be specific, documented, and tied to a Harbor tracking ID whenever possible. Immediate Action Items

1. All department leads must confirm checkpoint owners and backups by March 22. 2.

All department leads must confirm checkpoint owners and backups by March 22. 2. IT Support must validate access groups and manager permissions by March 25. 3. Customer Success leadership must retire legacy intake spreadsheet usage by March 31.
3. Customer Success leadership must retire legacy intake spreadsheet usage by March 31. 4. Product Operations must publish the current supported assay matrix to the Portal before go-live. 5.

Product Operations must publish the current supported assay matrix to the Portal before go-live. 5. Regional managers must confirm training completion status during the March 27 operations call. Closing Note

Project Harbor is not intended to add procedural overhead. Its purpose is to reduce ambiguity, improve handoffs, and make request status visible across teams without requiring repeated manual follow-up.

Closing Note

Project Harbor is not intended to add procedural overhead. Its purpose is to reduce ambiguity, improve handoffs, and make request status visible across teams without requiring repeated manual follow-up. The long-term success of Harbor will depend less on the software itself and more on consistent usage, disciplined documentation, and clear ownership at every checkpoint. Departments are expected to treat Q2 as a structured rollout period, with measured adjustments based on observed bottlenecks rather than ad hoc workarounds.
